Get Your Solar Proposal

Agricultural solar for rural Alberta

Farm Solar Installation in Alberta

Agricultural solar works best when the design reflects the farm’s seasonal loads, working buildings and long-term expansion plans.

Request a Farm Solar ProposalSee How It Works
Seasonal-load analysis
Rooftop and ground mount
Expansion-aware planning
Home/Farm Solar Installation in Alberta

Solar planned around the way the farm works.

Farms rarely use electricity in a flat, predictable way. We look at seasonal loads, working buildings, available land, service capacity and future expansion before shaping the project.

  • Barns, shops and working buildings
  • Irrigation, pumping and ventilation
  • Grain handling and seasonal equipment
  • Refrigeration, cold storage and greenhouses
  • Available land and access for ground mounts
  • Future buildings, equipment and electrical loads
Start Your Property Review

Clarity where the project decisions matter.

A strong solar project connects the energy profile, the property and the path to installation.

01

Built around farm loads

System planning starts with when and where energy is used across the operation—not a generic residential profile.

02

Flexible site options

Rooftops and ground mounts are compared against usable area, service location, access, working traffic and expansion plans.

03

Practical rural planning

Utility requirements, equipment schedules and installation logistics are addressed early so the concept fits the property.

The details that shape a durable farm solar project.

A rural site can offer excellent options, but the right design depends on load timing, service capacity and how the property needs to function for years to come.

  • Seasonal electricity patterns
  • Major equipment schedules
  • Rural service capacity
  • Roof and land suitability
  • Utility and approval requirements
  • Long-term farm expansion

Production, savings, incentives and timelines vary by site. A property-specific assessment is required.

A clear path from assessment to activation.

A farm-specific process that keeps operations and future growth in view.

01

Map the operation

Review bills, seasonal loads, equipment and expansion plans.

02

Compare site options

Assess working roofs, available land and electrical access.

03

Plan around the farm

Coordinate approvals, logistics and a practical installation window.

04

Install and support

Commission the system and establish ongoing monitoring.

Questions about farm solar

Straight answers help you decide whether the next step makes sense for your property.

Is a barn roof or a ground-mounted array better for my farm?+

It depends on roof condition, structural suitability, available land, shading, service location, working traffic and expansion plans. A site review can compare both options against the farm's actual loads.

Can solar be sized around irrigation or grain-drying loads?+

Those loads can be included, but their timing and seasonality matter. Equipment ratings, operating schedules and historical usage help determine how well demand aligns with solar production.

Will a ground mount interfere with farm equipment or livestock?+

Placement should account for machinery routes, snow management, drainage, fencing, setbacks and future land use. These practical constraints belong in the site plan from the beginning.

Can several farm buildings connect to one solar project?+

Potentially, but meter configuration, electrical distribution, distance between buildings and utility requirements affect the design. The electrical arrangement must be reviewed before assuming loads can be combined.

How much maintenance does a rural solar array require?+

Solar systems are generally low maintenance, but monitoring, visual inspections, vegetation control and access after severe weather are important. The handover should explain responsibilities and support contacts.

Should future farm expansion be included in the proposal?+

Yes. Planned shops, ventilation, pumping, refrigeration, electric equipment or service upgrades may influence system size and placement even if they are not operating today.

Your next step

Share your electricity usage and property details. We’ll organize the information needed to shape a clear solar proposal.
